THEME 01 Data Audit — CLL Spheroid EDA Pipeline Generated 2026-03-23 10:06
Theme 01 — Data Audit

Introduction

Purpose What is this audit and why does it exist?

Before any modelling begins, this audit establishes a complete inventory of all imaging and metadata assets in the CLL spheroid dataset. It answers three core questions:

  1. What annotated data do we have and is it of sufficient quality for training?
  2. What is the full scope of the raw unannotated imaging archive?
  3. What patient metadata can be reliably linked to the images?
Data Pools Two data sources — one local, one on the cluster
Annotated GT Pool — Local

51 brightfield images with hand-drawn binary segmentation masks, and 84 VID time-series images with instance masks (one integer per spheroid object). All images are stored on the local workstation.

These are the only labelled data available. They form the training and evaluation set for the segmentation model.

Figures 1–4 in this report concern this pool.

Full Raw Archive — Snellius Cluster

42,825 raw IncuCyte images across 19 experiments, covering three experiment categories: BTK inhibitor assays, drug screen assays, and refractory CLL samples. Scanned on the Snellius HPC cluster due to storage and compute constraints.

These images are unannotated. They represent the unlabelled pool for semi-supervised pre-training and temporal consistency evaluation.

Figures 5–6 in this report concern this archive.

Patient Metadata — Both Pools

Three Excel metadata files covering 33 unique patients (2,784 rows, 61 tracked columns). Includes patient demographics, IGHV mutational status, cytogenetics, clinical lab values, and per-well treatment conditions. Metadata is linked to both the annotated pool and the raw archive. Figures 7–11 cover the metadata.

Theme 01 — Data Audit

Audit Summary

Report Stage 1: Data Audit Report

Stage 1 — Data Audit Report

BF pool (primary GT pool)

  • Total images: 51
  • Images with mask: 51
  • Images without mask: 0
  • Metadata matched: 51 / 51
  • Duplicate hashes: 0
  • Wells covered: ['A4', 'A5', 'A6', 'B1', 'B10', 'B11', 'B2', 'B3', 'B4', 'B6', 'B7', 'B9', 'C1', 'C2', 'C3', 'C4', 'C6', 'D1', 'D10', 'D11', 'D12', 'D2', 'D3', 'D4', 'D6', 'D9', 'E4', 'E6', 'F3', 'F4', 'F6', 'G10', 'G4', 'G5', 'G6', 'G9', 'H11', 'H3', 'H4', 'H6', 'H9']
  • Timepoints covered: ['00d23h00m', '03d00h00m', '04d16h08m', '04d20h08m', '06d12h00m']

VID pool (secondary / time-series)

  • Total images: 84
  • Images with mask: 84
  • Images without mask: 0
  • Unique VIDs: ['VID1169', 'VID1873', 'VID2319', 'VID2359', 'VID2651']

Raw archive inventory (Snellius — full BTK/T/Refractory scan)

  • Total files: 42,825
  • Unique experiments: 19
  • Unique VIDs (named): 28
  • Files per category:

Btk 19,024

Drug_screen 18,605

Refractory 5,196

GT pool — raw timeseries coverage

  • GT images with matching raw series: 51 / 51
  • GT images with NO raw match: 0

Metadata summary

  • Unique patients (VIDs): 33
  • Sex distribution: {'Female': 18, 'Male': 15}
  • Age: median=66 range=50–85
  • IGHV: {'Mutated': 21, 'Unmutated': 6, 'Polyclonal': 1}
  • Columns <50% filled (43): Hb (mmol/L), Thrombocytes (G/L), %CD25high (CLL), %CD25high (CD4), %CD25high (CD8), gMFI CD25 (CLL), gMFI CD5 (CLL), gMFI CXCR4 (CLL), gMFI CD40 (CLL), %CD95high (CLL)…

QC issues — BF pool (sampled 10 pairs)

  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID3201_C4_1_04d16h08mL.jpg
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID3201_C4_1_04d16h08mL_masks.tif
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID3201_D4_1_04d20h08mL.jpg
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID3201_D4_1_04d20h08mL_masks.tif
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID3201_F3_1_04d20h08mL.jpg
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID3201_F3_1_04d20h08mL_masks.tif

QC issues — VID pool (sampled 10 pairs)

  • NON_BINARY_VALUES: [0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7] — VID2651_A6_1_01d18h00m_masks.tif
  • NON_BINARY_VALUES: [0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7] — VID2651_B3_1_00d06h00m_masks.tif
  • NON_BINARY_VALUES: [0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7] — VID2651_C4_1_05d12h00m_masks.tif
  • NON_BINARY_VALUES: [0 1 2 3 4 5 6] — VID1169_F7_1_03d12h00m_masks.tif
  • NON_BINARY_VALUES: [0 1 2 3 4 5] — VID1873_H4_1_03d07h00m_masks.tif
  • NON_BINARY_VALUES: [0 1 2 3] — VID1169_B10_1_02d12h00m_masks.tif
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID2651_A6_1_01d18h00m.jpg
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID2651_A6_1_01d18h00m_masks.tif
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID2651_B3_1_00d06h00m.jpg
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID2651_B3_1_00d06h00m_masks.tif
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID2651_C4_1_05d12h00m.jpg
  • WRONG_SHAPE: (960, 1280) (expected (1152, 1536)) — VID2651_C4_1_05d12h00m_masks.tif

Notes

  • BF pool masks are binary (0/255). Fragment count = connected components.
  • VID pool masks are instance-labeled (0,1,2,...). Count = unique non-zero values.
  • BF images come from different drug-screen experiments (different patients/VIDs).

They are cross-sectional — NO temporal autocorrelation within this pool.

  • VID pool: same well imaged at multiple timepoints → temporal autocorrelation risk.

Do NOT treat VID images from the same well as independent observations.

  • Raw archive (BF-prefix files in 2nd transfer) lack a VID in the filename.

GT coverage matching falls back to Well + timepoint for those images.

  • Raw archive figures are from the full Snellius scan (BTK + T + Refractory categories).

GT pool figures are from the local annotated dataset scan.

Reference Data Files Overview
ExtensionDescriptionPool / Role
.jpg / .jpeg Brightfield microscopy images (RGB, 8-bit), exported by IncuCyte software. BF pool (GT) · VID pool (time-series)
.tif (binary) Binary segmentation mask — pixel 0 = background, 255 = spheroid. Hand-annotated per image. BF pool — ground-truth masks
.tif (instance) Instance-label mask — each unique integer (1, 2, 3…) identifies one distinct spheroid object. VID pool — instance masks
.xlsx Excel metadata files: patient demographics, treatment conditions, clinical lab values, cytogenetics per well. Metadata — 3 files (btk · drug_screen · refractory)
.tif (raw archive) Raw unprocessed IncuCyte exports (~102k files). Not annotated. Used for semi-supervised pre-training and temporal analysis. Raw archive — two data transfers
.csv Pipeline-generated manifests, summaries, and QC tables produced by this audit. Pipeline outputs
Domain Domain Knowledge Glossary

Biological and technical terms used throughout this report.

BF (Brightfield)
Imaging mode using transmitted white light. Spheroids appear as dark circular structures against a bright background. No dye required.
VID
Video/Experiment identifier — a unique numeric ID for each IncuCyte imaging run. Format: VID followed by 4 digits (e.g. VID1797).
GT (Ground Truth)
Manually annotated binary mask indicating the true spheroid boundary. 51 GT pairs exist in this dataset.
Binary mask
An image where pixel value 255 = spheroid foreground and 0 = background. Used for BF pool annotations.
Instance mask
A mask where each distinct spheroid object has a unique integer label (1, 2, 3…). Used for VID pool annotations to enable object counting.
IncuCyte
Sartorius IncuCyte S3: automated live-cell imaging system. Photographs spheroids in 96-well plates at regular intervals over days.
Spheroid
A 3-D aggregate of CLL cells grown in suspension culture, mimicking the tumour micro-environment in vitro.
CLL
Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia — the most common adult leukaemia in Western countries. B-lymphocytes accumulate in blood, bone marrow, and lymphoid tissues.
Well
One compartment of a 96-well microplate (labelled A1–H12). Each well contains one patient sample under one drug condition.
Timepoint
The time after experiment start at which an image was captured. Format: DDdHHhMMm. E.g. 03d00h00m = 72 h post-seeding.
IGHV
Immunoglobulin Heavy chain Variable gene. Mutated IGHV → better prognosis; unmutated → more aggressive CLL with reduced drug response.
Rai Stage
Clinical staging for CLL severity (0–4). Stage 0 = lymphocytosis only; Stage 4 = thrombocytopenia. Higher stages = more advanced disease.
del13q14 / del17p13
Common chromosomal deletions in CLL. del17p13 (TP53 locus) confers the worst prognosis and resistance to many therapies.
BTK inhibitor
Drugs blocking Bruton's Tyrosine Kinase (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib). First-line targeted therapy for CLL.
BCL-2 inhibitor
Drugs blocking the BCL-2 survival protein (venetoclax/ABT-199). Highly effective in CLL, especially combined with BTK inhibitors.
MEK inhibitor
Drugs blocking the MEK kinase in the MAPK/ERK pathway. Used here in exploratory drug-screen conditions.
WRONG_SHAPE
QC flag: image resolution does not match the expected 1152×1536 px standard. Affects ~10 images exported at 960×1280 (different camera setting).
NON_BINARY_VALUES
QC flag on VID pool masks: pixel values include 0,1,2,3… (instance labels). This is expected — each integer identifies one spheroid object.
LOPO
Leave-One-Patient-Out cross-validation. Required here because multiple images come from the same patient; random splits would leak patient identity across train/test.
Semi-supervised learning
ML strategy combining a small labelled set (51 GT pairs) with the large unlabelled archive (~102k images) to improve model generalisation.
Theme 01

Figures

Annotated GT Pool

Figures 1–4 concern the manually annotated GT pool (local machine). 51 brightfield images + 84 VID time-series images with expert segmentation masks.

Figure 01 Dataset Overview
dataset_overview.png

Annotated BF (BrightField) Image Data Overview

How to read

There are two folders with manually annotated spheroids as binary masks serving as the

ground truth. BF and VID in this context refers to the prefix of raw spheroids within each folder.

Left panel: stacked bar showing how many images in each pool have a ground-truth (GT)

mask (teal) vs no mask (gold).

Right panel: pie showing how many BF images with GT could be matched to a treatment label in

the metadata Excel files.

Key findings

  • BF pool: 51 images, 51/51 (100%) have GT masks.
  • VID pool: 84 images, 84/84 (100%) have instance masks.
  • Metadata match: 51/51 BF images have a treatment label, meaning 100% of data is included

in the metadata.

What is remarkable

The GT pool linked to available metadata.

Implications

  • The 51 BF GT pairs are ready to be included for segmentation model training/evaluation.
  • No label-leakage risk: every image has both a mask and a treatment label.
  • The raw archive (~100k files) represents the unlabelled deployment pool —

the gap between these pools is >2000×, which motivates semi-supervised approaches.

Figure 02 BF Pool: Treatment Distribution
bf_treatment_distribution.png

Treatment Distribution of BF-well GT

How to read

Horizontal bar chart. Each bar is one drug treatment condition; bar length = number

of GT images under that condition. Teal = untreated controls; gold = drug-treated.

Key findings

  • Top treatments: trametinib (8), PD098060 (7), S-63845 (6).
  • Drug classes represented: MEK inhibitors (4 drugs),

BCL-2 family inhibitors (3 drugs),

BTK/SYK pathway (2 drugs).

  • Untreated controls in GT pool: NO.

What is remarkable

The GT pool contains zero untreated wells. All 51 images come from drug-treated

conditions. The MEK inhibitor trametinib is the most represented single drug (8

images), followed by MEK inhibitor PD098060 (7) and BCL-2 family drugs S-63845 and

PD098059 (6 each). The spread across BCL-2, MEK, and BTK/SYK pathways is deliberate

(these are the major CLL therapeutic axes), but the lack of available GT for the control

group is a bias.

Implications

  • A segmentation model trained only on these 51 images has never seen an untreated

CLL spheroid. Drug-induced morphological changes (disrupted spheroid compactness,

fragmentation) could possibly baked into the training distribution.

  • Recommend**: include untreated wells from the raw archive as held-out test images

to verify model generalisation to unperturbed spheroids.

  • Class imbalance within drug treatments (8 trametinib vs 1 MALTi) may mean the model

performs better on MEK-inhibited morphologies than on single-represented drugs, depending

on the model choice.

Figure 03 BF Pool: Timepoint Coverage
bf_timepoints.png

bf_timepoints.png

How to read

Horizontal bar chart. Each bar is one imaging timepoint (format: ddDhhHmmM);

bar length = number of GT images at that timepoint.

Key findings

  • Timepoints covered: ['00d23h00m', '03d00h00m', '04d16h08m', '04d20h08m', '06d12h00m']
  • Image counts per timepoint: {'00d23h00m': 16, '04d20h08m': 14, '03d00h00m': 10, '06d12h00m': 10, '04d16h08m': 1}
  • Earliest: ~24 h (16 images). Latest: ~6.5 days (10 images).

What is remarkable

Annotations are not uniformly distributed across time. The ~24h timepoint

dominates (16 images, 31%), followed by the

~day 4-5 timepoints. Only 1 image exists at the 04d16h08m timepoint, making it an

isolated singleton. Timepoints correspond to IncuCyte scan schedules rather than

biological milestones.

Implications

  • Temporal bias: a segmentation model evaluated at the ~24h timepoint will be tested

on the most represented morphology. Performance may be worse at later timepoints

(often larger and more fragmented spheroids).

  • The 04d16h08m singleton should be excluded from hold-out test splits, when supervised

learning is applied, as it cannot be representative on its own.

  • Raw timeseries data (73+ timepoints for some VIDs) could be used to expand

annotation coverage at underrepresented timepoints via active learning.

Figure 04 GT Pairs: Raw Timeseries Coverage
raw_gt_coverage.png

raw_gt_coverage.png

How to read

Left panel: horizontal bar per GT image, bar length = number of raw TIF files

matched (same VID + well). Teal = match found; cherry = no match.

Right panel: pie summarising how many of the 51 GT pairs have vs lack a raw series.

Key findings

  • GT pairs with raw timeseries: 51/51 (100%).
  • Raw timepoints per matched GT image range from 23 to 73.
  • Timeseries depth by VID: {'VID1087': 23, 'VID1797': 73, 'VID2356': 24, 'VID2357': 24, 'VID3201': 31}
  • All matches are in the Drug_screen category.

What is remarkable

Every single annotated GT image has a full raw timeseries in the archive.

This is not coincidental — the GT images were selected from IncuCyte exports and the

raw archive preserves those exact exports. VID1797 provides the richest temporal

context with 73 timepoints (~1 scan per 1.2h over 3.5 days) for each of its

10 annotated wells. The minimum coverage (VID2356/VID2357) is 24 timepoints.

Implications

  • Every GT annotation can be extended into a temporal sequence of 23-73 frames.

This enables: (a) temporal segmentation evaluation (is the model consistent over

time?), (b) using T-1 frames as context input, (c) pseudo-labelling adjacent frames

to expand the training set cheaply.

  • The 24-timepoint series (VID2356/VID2357 — BH3 mimetics ABT-199, S-63845,

A-1331852) cover a 24h window — sufficient to capture early drug response

(spheroid disruption typically begins within 6-12h of BCL-2 inhibitor treatment).

  • Action item**: extract the ±3 frame window around each GT annotation and inspect

visually to confirm the annotated frame is representative and not an artefact.

Full Raw Archive

Figures 5–6 concern the full unannotated raw archive scanned on the Snellius cluster. 42,825 IncuCyte images across 19 experiments (BTK, Drug Screen, Refractory categories).

Figure 05 Raw Archive: Overview
raw_archive_overview.png

The Raw Unannotated Data: Overview

How to read

Left panel: stacked bar,where each bar is one data transfer batch (1st vs 2nd), stacked

segments represent experiment categories (Btk / Drug_screen / NK / Refractory / T-cell).

Right panel: pie chart of all 42,825 files pooled across both transfers,

showing the fractional share of each experiment type.

Key findings

  • Total raw files: 42,825
  • By transfer: {'2nd_transfer': 1344, 'single': 41481}
  • By category (top 3): {'Btk': 19024, 'Drug_screen': 18605, 'Refractory': 5196}
  • Dominant category: Btk with 19,024 files (44% of archive).
  • GT pool (51 images) represents 0.0% of the raw archive.

What is remarkable

The annotated GT pool is a 0.05% sample of the full imaging data. The T-cell

experiment category (2nd transfer) alone has 18,605+

files, dwarfing the entire annotated pool.The 2nd transfer contributes nearly

half the archive despite covering fewer named experiments, because it uses

umbrella directories (Btk/, T/) that aggregate multiple patients and timepoints.

Implications

  • Enormous unlabelled pool (~102k images) → strong motivation for semi-supervised

or self-supervised pre-training before fine-tuning on the 51 GT pairs.

  • The category imbalance (T-cell dominant) means any random sampling of unlabelled

data for pre-training will over-represent T-cell assay morphologies.

  • The 2nd transfer archiving convention (no VID in filename for BF-prefix files)

makes systematic linkage to patient metadata harder and should be resolved in

future data collection.

Figure 06 Raw Archive: Top Experiments
raw_experiments_barchart.png

Barchart of the Top Experiments Found in the Raw Archive

How to read

Horizontal bar chart, longest bars at the bottom. Each bar is one experiment

directory (name truncated to 50 chars). Colour indicates experiment class:

teal = Btk, gold = Drug screen, cherry = Refractory, teal-light = NK/T-cell/Other.

Bar length = total number of TIF/JPG files in that directory.

Key findings

  • Top 5 experiments by file count:

1. 20240927_JLR36_T-cells_Killing assay exp 1: 5,568 files | 48 wells | 115 timepoints

2. 20241018_JLR38_T-cells_Killing assay_exp 2: 4,230 files | 90 wells | 47 timepoints

3. 20211025 BTKi proliferation-adhesion: 4,032 files | 96 wells | 14 timepoints

4. BD22 20211022 CD40 in immigrants and emigrants: 3,787 files | 60 wells | 15 timepoints

5. FD035_20240705_TCK pre-formed spheroid_allogenic k: 3,344 files | 88 wells | 26 timepoints

  • Densest temporal sampling: 20240927_JLR36_T-cells_Killing assay exp 1 with 115 unique timepoints.
  • Most experiments use 96 wells (full plate), with a few partial plates.

What is remarkable

The top two entries are directory-level aggregates from the 2nd transfer (Btk/

and T/ folders containing all sub-experiments), rather than individual assay runs.

The largest single-experiment timeseries is the 20230209 Spheroid formation assay

with 73 timepoints across 84 wells and has the richest temporal coverage in the dataset

and provides the source of 10 of the 51 GT annotations.

Implications

  • The densely-sampled experiments (73 timepoints, 1 image every ~1.2h) are ideal

for evaluating temporal consistency of segmentation models.

  • Experiments with only 1 timepoint (e.g. 20230802_JLR18 with a single scan) are

likely early-termination or failed runs and should be excluded from temporal analysis.

  • The 2nd transfer aggregation makes it impossible to distinguish individual patient

experiments without parsing the internal subdirectory structure.

Patient Metadata

Figures 7–11 cover the three metadata Excel files: 33 unique patients, 2,784 rows, 61 tracked columns.

Figure 07 Metadata: Completeness
metadata_completeness.png

Metadata Completeness

How to read

Horizontal bar chart. Each row is one tracked metadata column, labelled with its

domain group prefix (e.g. [Cytogen] del13q14). Bar length = % of rows in

metadata_combined.csv that have a non-empty value for that column.

Dashed lines mark the 50% (cherry) and 90% (teal-dark) thresholds.

Colours encode the domain group (see legend).

Key findings

  • Mean fill rate across 61 tracked columns: 28.6%.
  • Completely empty columns (0%): ['delNOTCH1', 'delMYD88', 'EZH2']
  • Critically sparse columns (<20% fill): ['Hb (mmol/L)', 'Thrombocytes (G/L)', 'gMFI CD5 (CLL)', 'gMFI CXCR4 (CLL)', 'gMFI CD40 (CLL)', 'gMFI Bcl-2 (CLL)', 'gMFI Bcl-XL (CLL)', 'gMFI Mcl-1 (CLL)']…
  • Well-filled columns (≥70%): ['Sex', 'Birth year', 'Age', 'Tissue', 'WBC (G/L)', 'Stimulation']
  • Hb (mmol/L) and Thrombocytes (G/L): only 12.9% filled.

What is remarkable

Three cytogenetics columns: delNOTCH1, delMYD88, EZH2 are **completely

empty** (0%). They exist in the metadata template but were never populated.

Similarly, the BCL-2 protein expression markers (gMFI Bcl-2, Bcl-XL, Mcl-1) are

filled at only 4.6%, despite being arguably the most drug-relevant biomarkers

given the BCL-2 inhibitor drugs in the GT pool [SOURCE]. Hb and thrombocytes fall to 12.9%

because they were only collected in one of the three metadata files.

Implications

  • Do not use** delNOTCH1, delMYD88, EZH2, gMFI CD40 for patient stratification as

no data exists.

  • BCL-2 family protein data (Bcl-2, Bcl-XL, Mcl-1 gMFI) is available for only

~130 wells, which could be used as supplementary validation, not as a primary covariate.

  • For ML covariate selection, restrict to columns with ≥50% fill: IGHV (59%),

Sex (76%), Age (76%), WBC (71%), %Lymphocytes (62%), %CD5+CD19+ (65%).

  • Cytogenetic marker analysis should pool del13q14/del17p13/del11q22/trisomy12

(20-30% fill) into a binary "any aberration" variable to reduce sparsity.

Figure 08 Metadata: Patient Demographics
metadata_demographics.png

Metadata Patient Demographics

How to read

2×2 panel:

  • Top-left: histogram of patient age at sample collection. Cherry dashed line = median.
  • Top-right: bar chart of sex distribution.
  • Bottom-left: bar chart of Rai stage (0–4 scale of CLL disease burden).
  • Bottom-right: bar chart of patients per experiment class (btk / drug_screen / refractory).

All panels use unique patients (one row per VID).

Key findings

  • 33 unique patients (VID-level deduplication).
  • Age: median = 66y, range 50–85y.
  • Sex: {'Female': 18, 'Male': 15}
  • Rai stage: {'0.0': 10, '2.0': 5, '1.0': 4, '4.0': 1, '3.0': 1}
  • Source breakdown: {'drug_screen': 16, 'btk': 14, 'refractory': 3}
  • Patients appearing under multiple VIDs (same Volgnummer): {234.0: 4, 706.0: 3, 1358.0: 2, 782.0: 2, 1199.0: 2, 2891.0: 2, 1043.0: 2}

What is remarkable

Only 33 unique patients generate 2,784 metadata rows, which on average is

84 rows per patient. This is because each well in each experiment

has its own row. Some patients appear under multiple VID numbers (e.g. Volgnummer

706/234) reflecting repeated sampling across different experiments. The cohort is

elderly (median 66y, consistent with CLL epidemiology) and

skewed female.

Implications

  • Patient-level cross-validation is mandatory.** With only 33 patients, a

random 80/20 image split would leak patients across train and test and as a result will inflate

metrics. Use leave-one-patient-out (LOPO) or grouped k-fold by VID.

  • The Rai stage distribution (if available for ≥55% of patients) covers early to

late disease — good for generalisation testing but the small n per stage limits

stratified analysis.

  • Repeated patients across VIDs should be treated as the same biological unit in

cross-validation; do not split them across folds.

Figure 09 Metadata: IGHV and Cytogenetics
metadata_ighv_genetics.png

Metadata: IGHV Genetics

How to read

Left panel: horizontal bar chart of IGHV mutational status counts (unique patients).

Teal = mutated (better prognosis); cherry = unmutated (worse prognosis); gold = other.

Right panel: horizontal bar chart of cytogenetic aberration prevalence as % of

patients for whom that marker was tested. Cherry dashed line at 30%, grey at 10%.

Key findings

  • IGHV status distribution: {'Mutated': 21, 'Unmutated': 6, 'Polyclonal': 1}
  • Top cytogenetic aberrations by prevalence: [('delATM', 100.0), ('trisomy19', 100.0), ('del13q14', 87.5), ('trisomy12', 53.3), ('del11q22', 41.7)]

What is remarkable

The cohort has a mutated IGHV predominance, this is by design (drug screen

experiments preferentially enroll CLL patients for whom novel agents are relevant,

and mutated-IGHV patients are a major target). However, unmutated IGHV CLL is

clinically more aggressive and drug-resistant and these patients are essential for

testing whether the segmentation model and downstream response predictions generalise

to the harder clinical scenario.

The cytogenetic profile shows del13q14 (most common CLL deletion) and trisomy12 as

the leading aberrations. del17p13 (TP53 loss) and del11q22 (ATM loss) are also

represented, both confer poor prognosis and predict reduced ibrutinib benefit.

Implications

  • Stratify any model evaluation by IGHV status.** Unmutated IGHV patients may

show fundamentally different spheroid morphologies (more proliferative, less

structured) compared to mutated cases.

  • del17p13 patients in the drug screen dataset are likely to show different

responses to BTK inhibitors as their imaging data should be annotated and

analysed separately to detect morphological resistance signatures.

  • IGHV data is missing for ~41% of patients meaning imputation or exclusion must be

decided before any IGHV-stratified analysis.

Figure 10 Metadata: Clinical Lab Values
metadata_clinical_boxplots.png

metadata_clinical_boxplots.png

How to read

One boxplot panel per clinical variable (WBC, % Lymphocytes, Hb, Thrombocytes,

Viability), split by experiment class (btk / drug_screen / refractory) on the x-axis.

Box = IQR (25th–75th percentile); horizontal bar = median; whiskers = 1.5×IQR;

dots = outliers. Colours: teal = btk, gold = drug_screen, cherry = refractory.

Note: Hb and Thrombocytes are only 12.9% filled, meaning caution with interpretation.

Key findings

  • WBC: 20.8 G/L median

(range 9.3–2177.0).

  • % Lymphocytes: 71.4% median.
  • Viability: 71.1% median

(range 8.0–95.4%).

What is remarkable

Viability is particularly important: low viability at the time of sample

preparation means a high proportion of dead cells enter the spheroid assay, which

directly affects spheroid formation efficiency and compactness. A viability of

<70% is generally considered problematic for spheroid assays. The range in this

cohort spans from very low to 100%, which is a major confounding variable.

WBC and lymphocyte percentage reflect disease burden at sample collection. High WBC

(>50 G/L) patients have more CLL cells available for seeding, potentially forming

larger or more numerous spheroids even without drug treatment.

Implications

  • Viability must be included as a covariate** in any morphological analysis.

It also leads to consider excluding or flagging experiments with viability <60%.

  • WBC and lymphocyte count could explain variance in baseline spheroid size, implicating to

normalise sphere metrics per-patient rather than across the full cohort.

  • The Refractory class likely has distinct clinical profiles (higher disease burden,

prior treatment exposure). Boxplots across classes should be compared

to detect systematic differences that could confound drug effect estimates..

Figure 11 Metadata: Treatment Distribution
metadata_treatment_dist.png

Metadata Treatment Distribution

How to read

One horizontal-bar subplot per experiment class (btk / drug_screen / refractory).

Each bar is one treatment condition; bar length = number of wells (rows) with that

treatment across all patients in that class. Teal = untreated controls;

gold = BTK inhibitors; cherry = all other treatments.

Key findings

  • Treatment distribution by source class: {'btk': {'ibrutinib': 165, 'ABT-199': 95, 'acalabrutinib': 83}, 'drug_screen': {'ABT-199': 68, 'trametinib': 65, 'S-63845': 56}, 'refractory': {'ibrutinib': 59, 'CONFIDENTIAL': 37, 'acalabrutinib': 20}}
  • Total untreated control rows across all classes: 64

What is remarkable

The btk class contains the most balanced treatment representation, with both

ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor) and untreated controls — enabling direct within-experiment

comparisons. The drug_screen class covers the widest pharmacological space:

MEK inhibitors (trametinib, PD098059, U0126), BCL-2 family agents (ABT-199,

S-63845, A-1331852), and CXCR4/MALTi compounds. The refractory class has

fewer unique treatments, consistent with its focus on characterising samples from

patients who have already failed standard therapies rather than testing novel drugs.

Implications

  • The btk class can serve as an internal validation set for BTK inhibitor response

signatures because it has paired treated/untreated data.

  • The drug_screen class is the primary discovery set for treatment-specific

morphological signatures.

  • Before comparing spheroid features across classes, verify that untreated controls

look similar across classes as any systematic difference would suggest batch effects

or patient-selection confounding rather than drug effects.

  • The GT pool (51 images) samples only the drug_screen class** which mean the btk

and refractory morphologies are entirely unrepresented in the annotated pool.

Theme 01

Key Tables
